core_behavior: Accept file uploads from authenticated users.
Validate uploaded files for type, size, and content.
Store uploaded files securely on the server.
Provide a unique identifier for each uploaded file.
Allow users to retrieve and download their uploaded files.
inputs: File: The file to be uploaded, which can be of various types (e.g., images, documents, videos).
User authentication credentials: Username and password or other authentication tokens to verify the user's identity.
File metadata: Optional metadata associated with the file, such as filename, description, and tags.
outputs: Upload success or failure notification: A message indicating whether the file was uploaded successfully or not.
File identifier: A unique identifier for the uploaded file, which can be used to retrieve or download the file.
File download: The uploaded file, which can be downloaded by the user.
assumptions: The system will only allow uploading files with specific extensions (e.g., .jpg, .pdf, .docx) to prevent malicious file uploads.
The system will impose a maximum file size limit (e.g., 10 MB) to prevent large file uploads.
The system will store uploaded files on a secure, dedicated file server.
The system will use a secure authentication mechanism, such as OAuth or OpenID Connect, to verify user identities.
The system will encrypt uploaded files at rest and in transit using a secure encryption algorithm (e.g., AES-256).
failure_modes: If the file upload fails due to network issues or server errors, the system will notify the user and provide an option to retry the upload.
If the uploaded file has an invalid or unsupported type, the system will notify the user and prevent the upload.
If the uploaded file exceeds the maximum size limit, the system will notify the user and prevent the upload.
If the user's authentication credentials are invalid or expired, the system will prevent the file upload and prompt the user to re-authenticate.
edge_cases: The system will prevent uploading zero-byte files to prevent potential security vulnerabilities.
The system will handle files with no extension by using the file's MIME type to determine its type.
The system will handle files with multiple extensions (e.g., file.tar.gz) by using the last extension to determine its type.
The system will handle concurrent file uploads from multiple users by using a thread-safe and queue-based upload mechanism.
security_constraints: The system will encrypt uploaded files at rest and in transit using a secure encryption algorithm (e.g., AES-256).
The system will implement role-based access control to ensure that only authorized users can upload, retrieve, and download files.
The system will use a secure authentication mechanism, such as OAuth or OpenID Connect, to verify user identities.
The system will validate user input, including file uploads, to prevent security vulnerabilities such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS).
performance_constraints: The system will support file uploads at a minimum speed of 1 MB/s.
The system will support file downloads at a minimum speed of 1 MB/s.
The system will support a minimum of 10 concurrent file uploads without significant performance degradation.
The system will have a minimum storage capacity of 100 GB to store uploaded files.
